subsections.title: IoC, DI & Spring Beans
subsections.keyRule: IoC (Inversion of Control) = Spring creates and manages your objects (beans). DI (Dependency Injection) = Spring injects dependencies for you — no 'new' needed. Bean scopes: Singleton (one per app), Prototype (new per request), Request (one per HTTP request), Session (one per HTTP session).

terms.what: IoC Container is Spring's object factory. It creates beans, wires dependencies, and manages bean lifecycle. ApplicationContext is the main IoC container interface.
terms.why: Without IoC, you manually create objects and pass dependencies — tight coupling, hard to test. IoC delegates creation to Spring — loose coupling, easy testing.
terms.how: @SpringBootApplication starts up ApplicationContext. Spring scans @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller — creates beans. You just @Autowired where needed.
terms.where: The foundation of every Spring application. All beans live here.
terms.when: Always let Spring manage your beans. Only create objects with 'new' for: simple value objects (DTO, entity), objects that should NOT be Spring-managed.
terms.issue: Trying to use @Autowired in a class created with 'new' — null injection. Spring cannot inject into manually-created objects.
terms.fix: Let Spring create and inject every bean. If you need a prototype bean, inject ApplicationContext and call getBean().

star.s: @Autowired repository is null in service.
star.a: Service was created with 'new Service()' in controller — not managed by Spring. Changed to @Autowired injection.
star.r: Dependencies injected correctly.

terms.what: Bean scope controls how many instances Spring creates. Singleton (default) = one instance for entire app. Prototype = new instance every time it is requested.
terms.why: Singleton is correct for stateless services. Prototype needed for stateful objects that should not be shared.
terms.how: @Scope('singleton') — default. @Scope('prototype'). @RequestScope — one per HTTP request. @SessionScope — one per HTTP session.
terms.where: Services and repositories: singleton. Request-scoped data (current user context): request scope. Shopping cart: session scope.
terms.when: Default to singleton for stateless beans. Use request scope for request-specific state. Never store mutable per-user data in a singleton — all users share it.
terms.issue: Field injection: private @Autowired OrderRepository repo — cannot be final, cannot be injected without Spring, hard to unit test (must use reflection or Spring test context).
terms.fix: Switch to constructor injection. Dependencies become final, clearly visible, and you can new OrderService(mockRepo) in unit tests — no Spring needed.

star.s: Unit tests are slow because they start full Spring context.
star.a: Services use constructor injection. Tests create services with 'new Service(mockDep)'.
star.r: Tests run in milliseconds without Spring. Bean Scopes

terms.what: These are stereotype annotations — they all ultimately extend @Component (which registers a bean). They communicate the role of the class.
terms.why: Same effect as @Component, but named annotations express intent. @Repository additionally adds exception translation (JDBC exceptions → Spring DataAccessException).
terms.how: @Repository on DAO classes. @Service on business logic. @Controller or @RestController on web endpoints. @Component for everything else.
terms.where: Every Spring bean in the app.
terms.when: Use the most specific annotation. @Service for services. @Repository for persistence. Never use bare @Component for classes that fit a specific role.
terms.issue: Using @Component everywhere — code compiles but loses clarity and @Repository exception translation.
terms.fix: Use correct stereotype for each class. Code reviews should flag wrong stereotype use.

star.s: JDBC SQLException from DAO not caught by @ExceptionHandler for DataAccessException.
star.a: DAO used @Component instead of @Repository — no Spring exception translation. Changed to @Repository.
star.r: JDBC exceptions auto- translated to DataAccessException.

subsections.title: Spring Boot & REST
subsections.keyRule: Spring Boot = Spring with auto-configuration and opinionated defaults. No XML config. Embedded Tomcat. Convention over configuration. @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody. REST: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove). Always return ResponseEntity with correct HTTP status.

terms.what: Spring Boot examines your classpath and auto-configures beans based on what it finds. Add Jackson on classpath = auto-configure JSON serialization. Add HikariCP + DB driver = auto-configure DataSource.
terms.why: No more XML config or dozens of @Bean definitions. Sensible defaults for 80% of use cases. Override when needed.
terms.how: @EnableAutoConfiguration (included in @SpringBootApplication) triggers class path scanning. Each AutoConfigure class has @ConditionalOn... to activate only when conditions met.
terms.where: Every Spring Boot application. Auto-configures: DataSource, JPA, Web MVC, Security, Actuator, Logging.
terms.when: Trust auto-config for standard setups. Override with properties (application.properties) first, then with explicit @Bean definitions when you need custom behavior.
terms.issue: Multiple DataSource configurations conflicting — two datasource beans found, Spring doesn't know which to use.
terms.fix: @Primary on the main DataSource bean, or @Qualifier to specify which to inject.

star.s: Spring Boot app needs two databases.
star.a: Defined second @Bean DataSource with @Qualifier('secondDb').
star.r: Two datasources, explicitly distinguished by qualifier.

terms.what: @RestController combines @Controller and @ResponseBody — every method returns data (as JSON) directly, not a view name. Mapping annotations route HTTP requests to methods.
terms.why: Clean way to build REST APIs. No boilerplate @ResponseBody on every method. Annotations map HTTP verbs and paths to Java methods.
terms.how: @GetMapping('/users/{id}'), @PostMapping('/users'), @PutMapping, @DeleteMapping, @PatchMapping. @PathVariable for URL params, @RequestParam for query params, @RequestBody for JSON body.
terms.where: Every REST API endpoint.
terms.when: Use @GetMapping for reads. @PostMapping for create (return 201). @DeleteMapping (return 204). Always validate input with @Valid.
terms.issue: Returning null from a REST endpoint — Spring returns 200 with empty body. Clients cannot distinguish 'found but empty' from 'not found'.
terms.fix: Return ResponseEntity<T>. Not found: return ResponseEntity.notFound().build() — HTTP 404. Success: return ResponseEntity.ok(data) — HTTP 200.

star.s: API returns 200 with null body when user not found. Clients confused.
star.a: Return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(new ErrorDto('User not found')).
star.r: Clear 404 response. Clients handle correctly.

terms.what: AOP (Aspect Oriented Programming) lets you add behavior (logging, security, transactions) to methods without modifying the method code itself — 'cross-cutting concerns'.
terms.why: Logging, timing, transactions, security checks are needed everywhere but should not pollute business logic code.
terms.how: @Aspect class with @Around, @Before, @After advice. @Pointcut defines which methods to intercept. Spring wraps the target bean in a proxy.
terms.where: @Transactional IS AOP. Spring Security IS AOP. @Cacheable IS AOP. You can add custom AOP for: audit logging, performance timing, retry logic.
terms.when: Use AOP for: logging method calls, measuring performance, retry, audit. Avoid for: business logic — that should be explicit.
terms.issue: AOP self-invocation problem: calling an AOP-intercepted method from the same class bypasses the proxy — @Transactional/@Cacheable won't work.
terms.fix: Call AOP-proxied methods from a different Spring bean. Or use AspectJ compile- time weaving to avoid proxy limitation.

star.s: Every service method needs execution time logging.
star.a: @Around AOP aspect measures time for all methods in @Service classes.
star.r: Performance logging added to 50 methods with one 15-line aspect.

terms.what: Spring Security handles authentication (who are you?) and authorization (what can you do?). Integrates with JWT, OAuth2, LDAP, Basic Auth.
terms.why: Security is complex — Spring Security handles it correctly so you don't have to. Handles CSRF, session fixation, clickjacking protections by default.
terms.how: @EnableWebSecurity + SecurityFilterChain bean. Authenticate via JWT filter or session. Authorize with .authorizeHttpRequests().requestMatchers('/admin').hasRole('ADMIN').
terms.where: Every production REST API. Secure endpoints by role, authenticated vs public.
terms.when: Add Spring Security from the start. Never build custom security from scratch. Use JWT for stateless REST APIs, session-based for web apps.
terms.issue: CORS blocking REST API calls from a front-end on a different domain — '403 Forbidden' or no response.
terms.fix: Add @CrossOrigin on controller or configure CorsConfigurationSource in SecurityFilterChain. Always specify exact origins in production — never '*'.

star.s: Mobile app cannot call backend API — CORS error.
star.a: Added CorsConfigurationSource bean allowing specific mobile app origin.
star.r: API accessible from mobile. Other origins still blocked.

subsections.title: Spring IoC Extended
subsections.keyRule: BeanFactory = minimal IoC (lazy). ApplicationContext = BeanFactory + events + AOP + messaging. @Configuration + @Bean = Java config. @Qualifier disambiguates. @Primary = default bean. @Lazy = lazy initialization. @Scope(prototype) = new instance every injection.

terms.what: @Configuration marks a class as a Spring configuration class. @Bean marks a method that returns a Spring bean — Spring manages its lifecycle.
terms.why: Programmatic bean definition — more flexible than component scanning. Lets you create beans from third-party classes you cannot annotate.
terms.how: @Configuration class AppConfig { @Bean DataSource dataSource() { HikariDataSource ds = new HikariDataSource(); ds.setJdbcUrl(url); return ds; } }
terms.where: Infrastructure beans (DataSource, RestTemplate, ObjectMapper, Kafka consumer factories), third-party library integration. TERM MEANING
terms.when: Use @Bean when you cannot add @Component to a class (third-party). Use @Component/@Service when you own the class.
terms.issue: @Bean method not called if called directly from another @Bean method — unless class is @Configuration (CGLIB proxied). Calling @Bean on @Component class = new instance each time.
terms.fix: Always use @Configuration (not @Component) for classes with @Bean methods that call each other. @Configuration's CGLIB proxy intercepts direct method calls.

star.s: Two @Bean methods share a DataSource — second creates a second DataSource instead of reusing first.
star.a: Class was @Component not @Configuration. Changed to @Configuration. CGLIB proxy ensures single DataSource.
star.r: Beans share one DataSource.

terms.what: @Primary: marks a bean as the default when multiple beans of same type exist. @Qualifier('name'): selects a specific bean by name when multiple exist. @Lazy: tells Spring to create the bean only when first accessed.
terms.why: Multiple implementations of same interface — need to inject the right one. @Lazy reduces startup time for expensive beans.
terms.how: @Primary @Service class SqlUserService. @Service class CacheUserService. @Autowired UserService service; // injects SqlUserService (primary). @Autowired @Qualifier('cacheUserService') UserService cached;
terms.where: Multiple DataSources, multiple MessageSources, multiple TransactionManagers, multiple implementations of same interface.
terms.when: @Primary for the most common implementation. @Qualifier for specific injection. @Lazy for expensive beans used infrequently.
terms.issue: @Primary not set when multiple beans of same type exist = UnsatisfiedDependencyException — no unique bean.
terms.fix: Set @Primary on the default implementation, or use @Qualifier at every injection point.

star.s: Two DataSource beans — Primary (MySQL) and Secondary (analytics).
star.a: @Primary on MySQL DataSource. @Qualifier('analytics') where analytics is needed.
star.r: Correct DB used everywhere.

terms.what: @ConditionalOnClass: bean created only if certain class is on classpath. @ConditionalOnProperty: bean created only if a property has a specific value. Both used in Spring Boot auto-configuration.
terms.why: Auto-configuration is conditional — Redis bean only created if Jedis/Lettuce is on classpath. Feature toggle via property.
terms.how: @ConditionalOnClass(RedisTemplate.class) @Bean RedisCache redisCache(...). @ConditionalOnProperty(name='feature.enabled', havingValue='true') @Bean FeatureBean featureBean(...)
terms.where: Auto-configuration classes, feature flags, environment-specific beans.
terms.when: Use @ConditionalOnProperty for feature flags and environment-specific behavior. @ConditionalOnClass for optional integrations.
terms.issue: @ConditionalOnProperty default behavior when property is missing depends on 'matchIfMissing' attribute (default=false = bean NOT created).
terms.fix: Set matchIfMissing=true if you want the bean by default and disable it explicitly.

star.s: New feature must be toggleable without redeploy.
star.a: @ConditionalOnProperty(name='new.feature.enabled', havingValue='true', matchIfMissing=false). Set property in env config.
star.r: Feature enabled/disabled without code change.

terms.what: Spring Profiles activate different configurations for different environments (dev, test, prod). Beans/configs annotated with @Profile('prod') only active when 'prod' profile is active.
terms.why: Different environments need different settings: dev uses H2 DB, prod uses PostgreSQL. Profiles manage this without code changes.
terms.how: @Profile('dev') @Bean DataSource devDataSource(). @Profile('prod') @Bean DataSource prodDataSource(). Activate: spring.profiles.active=prod or SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod env var.
terms.where: DataSource configuration, external service URLs, email config (no real emails in dev), feature toggles.
terms.when: Always have at minimum dev and prod profiles. Consider test profile for integration tests.
terms.issue: Forgetting to set active profile in production — app runs with default (dev) settings — wrong DB, wrong credentials.
terms.fix: Make profile-sensitive beans fail if no profile active: @Profile('!default'). Always set SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE in deployment scripts.

star.s: Dev H2 database accidentally used in staging.
star.a: Added @Profile('dev') on H2 bean. @Profile('!dev') on real DB bean. Staging must set profile explicitly.
star.r: Wrong DB impossible by design.

terms.what: DispatcherServlet is Spring MVC's front controller — all HTTP requests go through it. HandlerMapping maps URLs to controller methods. HandlerAdapter invokes the method.
terms.why: Understanding the request lifecycle helps debug routing issues, interceptors, and custom filters.
terms.how: Request → DispatcherServlet → HandlerMapping (finds @RequestMapping method) → HandlerAdapter (invokes method) → View/JSON response.
terms.where: Embedded in every Spring Boot web application. configure via WebMvcConfigurer.
terms.when: Understand this for debugging 404s (HandlerMapping finds nothing), content negotiation issues, and custom interceptor placement.
terms.issue: Two @RequestMapping mappings for same URL — DispatcherServlet throws AmbiguousHandlerMappingException.
terms.fix: Ensure unique URL patterns. Use @RequestMapping with method attribute: @GetMapping vs @PostMapping.

star.s: New endpoint returns 404 despite being annotated correctly.
star.a: Controller missing @RestController — DispatcherServlet skips plain @Controller with no view. Added @RestController.
star.r: Endpoint found.

terms.what: HttpMessageConverter converts between Java objects and HTTP request/response body. Jackson's MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter handles JSON. Content negotiation selects format based on Accept header.
terms.why: @RequestBody and @ResponseBody use MessageConverters to serialize/deserialize. Content negotiation supports multiple formats (JSON, XML) from same endpoint.
terms.how: @RequestBody reads JSON → Java via Jackson. @ResponseBody writes Java → JSON via Jackson. Accept: application/json → Jackson. Accept: application/xml → JAXB.
terms.where: All @RestController endpoints. Configured via WebMvcConfigurer.configureMessageConverters().
terms.when: Configure converters when: adding XML support, customizing Jackson behavior (dates, nulls), supporting custom media types.
terms.issue: Jackson cannot deserialize unknown field → 400 error. Jackson fails on unrecognized properties by default.
terms.fix: @JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown=true) on DTO class, or configure ObjectMapper: mapper.configure(FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES, false).

star.s: API returns 400 when client sends extra fields in JSON.
star.a: Added @JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown=true) to request DTOs.
star.r: Extra fields ignored. Backward compatible API.

terms.what: @Valid triggers Bean Validation on @RequestBody. BindingResult captures validation errors as structured data — method still called. Without BindingResult, invalid input throws MethodArgumentNotValidException.
terms.why: @Valid alone throws exception on invalid input — no control over error response. BindingResult lets you inspect errors and return custom responses.
terms.how: @PostMapping('/user') ResponseEntity<?> create(@Valid @RequestBody UserDTO user, BindingResult result) { if(result.hasErrors()) return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(result.getAllErrors()); ... }
terms.where: REST API endpoints with request body validation.
terms.when: Use @Valid without BindingResult and handle MethodArgumentNotValidException globally with @ExceptionHandler for cleaner code. Use BindingResult only for complex per-field custom logic.
terms.issue: BindingResult must be the parameter immediately after the @Valid parameter — otherwise Spring throws exception.
terms.fix: Always: @PostMapping method(@Valid @RequestBody Dto dto, BindingResult result) — BindingResult must follow @Valid param directly.

star.s: API throws exception for every validation error — clients get stack trace.
star.a: Global @ExceptionHandler for MethodArgumentNotValidException. Returns 400 with field-error list.
star.r: Clean validation errors for all endpoints.

subsections.title: Spring AOP Extended
subsections.keyRule: AOP terms: Aspect = the class containing cross-cutting logic. Advice = the action (Before/After/Around). Pointcut = the expression that selects methods to intercept. JoinPoint = the intercepted method call. Weaving = applying aspects to target code. JDK proxy = for interfaces. CGLIB proxy = for classes.

terms.term: AOP Terms: Aspect, Advice, Pointcut, JoinPoint
terms.what: Aspect = class with cross-cutting logic (@Aspect). Advice = when to run (@Before, @After, @Around, @AfterReturning, @AfterThrowing). Pointcut = which methods to intercept. JoinPoint = the actual method being intercepted.
terms.why: Understanding AOP terminology helps you write correct aspects and debug proxy- related issues.

terms.what: JDK Dynamic Proxy: wraps an interface — target must implement an interface. CGLIB Proxy: subclasses the target class — no interface needed. Spring uses JDK proxy for interface-based beans, CGLIB for class-based.
terms.why: Understanding which proxy is used explains: why @Transactional fails on final methods (CGLIB cannot subclass final), why interface injection is needed with JDK proxy.
terms.how: JDK proxy: @Autowired UserService service; // UserService is interface. CGLIB: Spring Boot uses CGLIB by default (spring.aop.proxy-target-class=true).
terms.where: All Spring AOP features: @Transactional, @Cacheable, @Async, @Secured.
terms.when: Use interfaces for Spring beans — makes both JDK and CGLIB proxying work. Avoid final methods/classes in Spring beans.
terms.issue: CGLIB cannot subclass final class or override final method — @Transactional on final method = no transaction.
terms.fix: Remove final from methods that need AOP interception. Or use interfaces.

star.s: @Transactional on a method does nothing.
star.a: Method is final — CGLIB proxy cannot override it. Removed final.
star.r: Transaction applied correctly.

terms.what: @AfterReturning: runs after method returns successfully — can access return value. @AfterThrowing: runs after method throws exception — can access the exception.
terms.why: More targeted than @After — react to success or failure specifically. Common for audit logging: log what was returned or what went wrong.
terms.how: @AfterReturning(pointcut='...', returning='result') void logReturn(Object result) { log.info('Returned: {}', result); } @AfterThrowing(pointcut='...', throwing='ex') void logError(Exception ex) { log.error(ex.getMessage()); }
terms.where: Audit logging, metrics collection, error notification, result transformation (though prefer @Around for transformation).
terms.when: Use @AfterReturning to log success results. @AfterThrowing to log/handle exceptions. @Around for full control (modify input, output, or handle exceptions).
terms.issue: @AfterReturning cannot modify the return value — use @Around for that.
terms.fix: For result modification, use @Around: Object result = pjp.proceed(); return transformResult(result);

star.s: Audit trail needs to log what data was returned from each service method.
star.a: @AfterReturning aspect logs method signature + return value as JSON.
star.r: Complete audit trail with zero changes to service code.

subsections.title: Spring Transactions Extended
subsections.keyRule: Propagation types: REQUIRED (default, join or create), REQUIRES_NEW (always new, suspend current), NESTED (savepoint within current), SUPPORTS (join if exists, else no tx), MANDATORY (must exist), NEVER (must not exist), NOT_SUPPORTED (suspend current). rollbackFor: default unchecked only. Add rollbackFor=Exception.class for checked.

terms.what: Propagation defines what happens when a @Transactional method is called from another @Transactional context. 7 types: REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, NESTED, SUPPORTS, MANDATORY, NEVER, NOT_SUPPORTED.
terms.why: Correct propagation prevents: accidentally nesting transactions, losing rollbacks, or creating transactions where they don't belong.
terms.how: REQUIRED (default): join existing or create new. REQUIRES_NEW: always create new, suspend outer. NESTED: create savepoint within current (rolls back to savepoint on error). SUPPORTS: use existing if present, else no transaction.
terms.where: Any @Transactional method. Critical for: audit logging (should save even if main tx rolls back → REQUIRES_NEW), batch processing, and nested service calls.
terms.when: Use REQUIRED for most cases. REQUIRES_NEW for operations that must commit independently (audit, notification). NESTED for partial rollback within a transaction.
terms.issue: REQUIRED propagation: inner method rollback = outer transaction also rolled back — all work lost.
terms.fix: For operations that must succeed even if outer tx fails: use REQUIRES_NEW. This creates an independent transaction.

star.s: Audit log not saved when order transaction fails.
star.a: @Transactional(propagation=REQUIRES_NEW) on audit method.
star.r: Audit saves in its own transaction regardless of outer tx outcome.

terms.what: rollbackFor: which exceptions trigger rollback (default: RuntimeException only). noRollbackFor: exceptions that should NOT trigger rollback. readOnly=true: hint for performance optimization — no locks for reads.
terms.why: Checked exceptions don't rollback by default — common bug. readOnly=true lets DB optimize read-only queries (no write locks).
terms.how: @Transactional(rollbackFor=Exception.class) — rollback on any exception. @Transactional(noRollbackFor=StockWarningException.class) — don't rollback for warnings. @Transactional(readOnly=true) — for query methods.
terms.where: Service methods. readOnly=true on all query-only service methods.
terms.when: Add rollbackFor=Exception.class to services calling external APIs (checked exceptions possible). readOnly=true on all read-only service methods — free performance win.
terms.issue: @Transactional method catches a checked IOException and logs it — but default @Transactional would not have rolled back anyway even if it propagated.
terms.fix: Add rollbackFor=Exception.class to ensure checked exceptions also trigger rollback.

star.s: File import service doesn't rollback DB changes when file parse fails (checked IOException).
star.a: Added @Transactional(rollbackFor=Exception.class).
star.r: DB rolled back on any exception.

terms.what: TransactionTemplate provides programmatic transaction management — control transaction boundaries in code. PlatformTransactionManager is the underlying Spring transaction abstraction.
terms.why: @Transactional is declarative — less control. TransactionTemplate gives full programmatic control: start, execute, commit/rollback based on conditions.
terms.how: TransactionTemplate tmpl = new TransactionTemplate(txManager); tmpl.execute(status -> { dao.save(entity); return null; }); — throws exception = rollback. Returns = commit.
terms.where: Complex transaction logic, batch processing with per-item commits, programmatic rollback based on business conditions. TERM MEANING
terms.when: Use @Transactional for 95% of cases. Use TransactionTemplate when you need: programmatic rollback based on result, different transaction per loop iteration, conditional commit.
terms.issue: TransactionTemplate swallows RuntimeExceptions for rollback — the exception is rethrown after rollback.
terms.fix: Wrap TransactionTemplate.execute() in try-catch if you need to handle exceptions after rollback.

star.s: Batch processor must commit every 100 rows — single @Transactional commits all or none.
star.a: TransactionTemplate in loop, commits every 100 rows.
star.r: Batch can be resumed from last 100-row commit on failure.

subsections.title: Spring Security Extended
subsections.keyRule: Security chain: request → SecurityFilterChain → authenticate → authorize → resource. UserDetailsService loads user. PasswordEncoder hashes passwords. HttpSecurity configures access rules. @PreAuthorize/@Secured for method security. JWT = stateless token auth. OAuth2 = delegated authorization.

terms.what: UserDetailsService.loadUserByUsername() loads user from DB by username — returns UserDetails (username, password, authorities). PasswordEncoder hashes and verifies passwords (BCryptPasswordEncoder).
terms.why: Spring Security needs these to authenticate users. You provide the user data; Spring Security handles the authentication flow.
terms.how: @Service class MyUserDetails implements UserDetailsService { UserDetails loadUserByUsername(String username) { User u = userRepo.findByUsername(username); return new org.springframework.security.core.userdetails.User(u.getName(), u.getPassword(), u.getRoles()); } }
terms.where: Any Spring Security application. Custom authentication against your user DB.
terms.when: Implement UserDetailsService when you need custom user loading (from DB, LDAP, etc.). Use BCryptPasswordEncoder for password storage.
terms.issue: Storing plain-text passwords — security disaster. Old code using MD5 or SHA1 — not secure for passwords.
terms.fix: ALWAYS use BCryptPasswordEncoder. Never store plain text. On save: passwordEncoder.encode(rawPassword). On login: passwordEncoder.matches(raw, stored).

star.s: Code review — passwords stored as MD5 hash.
star.a: Changed to BCryptPasswordEncoder. Migration: on first login with old MD5, re-hash and save BCrypt.
star.r: Passwords progressively upgraded.

terms.what: HttpSecurity configures URL-level access rules in SecurityFilterChain. @PreAuthorize provides method-level security with SpEL. @Secured is simpler — list of required roles.
terms.why: Two levels: URL security for coarse-grained access, method security for fine- grained business logic.
terms.how: http.authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth.requestMatchers('/admin/**').hasRole('ADMIN').anyRequest().authenticated()); @PreAuthorize('hasRole(ADMIN) or #userId == authentication.name')
terms.where: All secured Spring Boot applications. @PreAuthorize on service/controller methods.
terms.when: Use HttpSecurity for URL-based access control. Use @PreAuthorize for business-logic security (user can only access their own resources).

terms.what: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a stateless token: header.payload.signature. Server creates JWT on login, client sends it in Authorization header. Server validates signature — no session storage needed.
terms.why: Stateless — scales horizontally. No session affinity required. Works for microservices and mobile apps.
terms.how: Login → server creates JWT signed with secret. Client sends: Authorization: Bearer <token>. Server validates signature + expiry. Extract user from token claims.
terms.where: REST APIs, microservices, mobile app backends, single-page applications.
terms.when: Use JWT for stateless APIs. Do NOT use JWT for sessions in traditional web apps where you need instant revocation.
terms.issue: JWTs cannot be instantly revoked — stolen token valid until expiry. Also, large payloads increase every request size.
terms.fix: Short expiry (15 min) + refresh token. Blacklist compromised tokens in Redis if instant revocation needed. Keep JWT payload minimal.

star.s: System needs scalable stateless auth across 10 microservices.
star.a: JWT auth at API Gateway. All services validate signature with shared public key.
star.r: No session affinity. Any service can validate any request.

terms.what: OAuth2: delegated authorization — 'Login with Google/GitHub' — third-party issues token. CSRF: Cross-Site Request Forgery protection — prevents malicious sites from making authenticated requests on behalf of users. SecurityContextHolder: thread- local storage of current authenticated user.
terms.why: OAuth2 for federated identity. CSRF for form-based apps. SecurityContextHolder for getting current user anywhere.
terms.how: OAuth2: spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client + application.yml config. CSRF: enabled by default for forms, disabled for stateless REST. SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() gets current user.
terms.where: OAuth2: SSO, social login. CSRF: form-based web apps. SecurityContextHolder: service methods needing current user.
terms.when: Disable CSRF for REST APIs (stateless, token auth). Enable for session-based web apps. Use SecurityContextHolder in service layer (avoid in domain layer).
terms.issue: CSRF disabled globally for REST but forms accidentally use same security config — forms become vulnerable.
terms.fix: Separate security config for API (stateless, no CSRF) and form (session, CSRF enabled).

star.s: Add 'Login with Google' to app.
star.a: spring-boot-starter- oauth2-client, configure google client-id/secret in yml, add /oauth2/login endpoint. Spring handles flow.
star.r: Google OAuth2 login working in 20 lines of config. 14. TESTING — JUnit 5, Mockito & Spring Boot Test

subsections.title: JUnit 5, Mockito & Spring Boot Test
subsections.keyRule: Unit test = test one class in isolation (mock dependencies). Integration test = test with real Spring context + real DB. @SpringBootTest = full context — slow. @WebMvcTest = web layer only. @DataJpaTest = JPA layer only. Test Pyramid: many unit tests, some integration, few E2E.

terms.what: JUnit 5 is the standard Java testing framework. Tests are methods annotated with @Test. Lifecycle: @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, @AfterAll.
terms.why: Structured, automated test execution. Tests document expected behavior. Catch regressions before production.
terms.how: @Test void shouldReturnUser() { User user = service.find(1L); assertNotNull(user); assertEquals('Alice', user.getName()); }. Use AssertJ for fluent assertions: assertThat(user.getName()).isEqualTo('Alice');
terms.where: Every class, every public method should have corresponding tests.
terms.when: Write tests before fixing bugs (prove the bug, then fix). Write tests before adding features (TDD). At minimum, test all business logic paths.
terms.issue: Tests that depend on execution order — one test pollutes state for the next.
terms.fix: Each test must be independent. Use @BeforeEach to reset state. Never share mutable state between tests.

star.s: Test suite passes alone but fails in certain order.
star.a: Found @BeforeAll initializing shared mutable list not reset between tests. Moved to @BeforeEach.
star.r: Tests independent — pass in any order.

terms.what: Mockito lets you replace real dependencies with fake ones (mocks) that return whatever you tell them to. Tests run without real DB, network, or external services.
terms.why: Real dependencies make tests slow (DB calls), unreliable (network down), and hard (you don't control external services).
terms.how: @Mock OrderRepository repo; @InjectMocks OrderService service; when(repo.findById(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.of(testOrder)); service.getOrder(1L); verify(repo).findById(1L);
terms.where: Every unit test that tests a class with dependencies.
terms.when: Mock all external dependencies in unit tests. Do NOT mock your own classes — if you need to, your design has too many responsibilities.
terms.issue: Mocking the class under test (the SUT) instead of its dependencies — you are testing the mock, not your code.
terms.fix: Only mock dependencies. The class you are testing is REAL. Dependencies injected into it are MOCKS.

star.s: Service test requires running database — too slow for CI.
star.a: Replaced @Autowired repository with @Mock. Used when().thenReturn() to define behavior.
star.r: Tests run in 50ms without DB.

terms.what: @SpringBootTest starts full Spring application context for integration tests. @WebMvcTest starts only the web layer (controllers + MVC config) — faster.
terms.why: @SpringBootTest tests real integration between layers. @WebMvcTest tests controllers without starting full app.
terms.how: @WebMvcTest(UserController.class) @AutoConfigureMockMvc — injects MockMvc. mockMvc.perform(get('/users/1')).andExpect(status().isOk()).andExpect(jsonPath('$.name').value('Al ice'));
terms.where: @WebMvcTest for controller tests. @DataJpaTest for repository tests. @SpringBootTest for full integration.
terms.when: Use @WebMvcTest for controller layer tests — fast and focused. Use @SpringBootTest only when you need to test the full integration.
terms.issue: @SpringBootTest starts full context — loads all beans, DB, everything. Very slow for large apps.

terms.what: @DataJpaTest starts only the JPA layer (entities, repositories, in-memory H2 DB) — no web layer, no service layer. Fast, focused repository tests.
terms.why: Tests your JPA queries and repository methods in isolation without full app startup.
terms.how: @DataJpaTest class UserRepositoryTest { @Autowired UserRepository repo; @Test void shouldFindByEmail() { repo.save(new User('Alice', 'a@b.com')); Optional<User> found = repo.findByEmail('a@b.com'); assertTrue(found.isPresent()); } }
terms.where: Every custom @Query or derived query method in Spring Data repositories.
terms.when: Use @DataJpaTest for repository tests. Tests run against H2 in-memory DB — no real DB needed.
terms.issue: @DataJpaTest uses H2 by default — SQL dialects differ. A query that works in MySQL might fail in H2.
terms.fix: For critical DB-specific queries, use @AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace=NONE) to test against the real DB (requires test DB setup).

star.s: Custom JPQL query untested — bug found in production.
star.a: @DataJpaTest with H2. Test each query method with multiple scenarios.
star.r: Query bugs caught before production.

terms.what: Test coverage measures what percentage of code is tested. High coverage alone doesn't mean good tests — quality of tests matters more than quantity.
terms.why: Tests document behavior, catch regressions, enable confident refactoring, and speed up debugging.
terms.how: Use JaCoCo for coverage reports. Aim for 80%+ on business logic. 100% is diminishing returns on trivial code.
terms.where: CI pipeline should fail if coverage drops below threshold. Focus on: service layer, domain logic, complex algorithms.
terms.when: Write tests as you write code (TDD) or immediately after. Never 'add tests later' — it rarely happens.
terms.issue: 100% coverage but tests only check happy path — all sad paths uncovered. Production failures in error scenarios.
terms.fix: Test edge cases: null input, empty list, boundary values, exception paths. Parameterized tests (@ParameterizedTest) cover multiple cases efficiently.

star.s: New developer asks what to test.
star.a: Test everything that contains logic. Happy path first, then edge cases (null, empty, boundary), then exception paths. Use TDD for new features.
star.r: Team builds test habits from the start.
